title: The Future of AI Is Here — What You NEED To Know
description: A $300 billion company. A surprise Zoom firing. A five-day corporate meltdown. In this video, Sandeep unpacks the jaw-dropping story of how OpenAI nearly imploded—and what it teaches us about power, leadership, and the future of AI. As a former CEO and board advisor, Sandeep shares the deeper lessons hiding beneath the headlines: how mission and money collide, why relationships matter more than résumés, and what we can all learn from the human failures behind the machines.
transcript:
This man built a $300 billion empire and
was fired by his own board on a Zoom
call. Within 120 hours, the company
nearly collapsed. I have spent years
building and advising tech companies as
a CEO, as a board member, and this story
surprised me the most. The lessons, they
show up in every job, every business,
every team. Power, trust, what to do,
what not to do as a leader. So much to
learn. Let's get started. Act one, the
120our implosion. The board tried to
kill the company to save it. It all
starts here. A Zoom call and a guest
badge. Friday, November 17th, 2023. Sam
Alman, the co-founder and CEO of OpenAI,
logs into his regular one-on-one with
Ilia Sutzk. He's OpenAI's chief
scientist and a board member. And it's
just another weekly call. But this time,
something is off. Two unexpected faces
appear on the call. Helen Toner and
Tasha Macaulay. They're both fellow

Ilia, never known to mince words, says,

reasoning vague at best. Altman had not

communications. That's what they said.
But within minutes, the news spreads. By

board even floats a merger with

names a new interim CEO, EMTT Sheer. The

Employees are angry. A lot of tech

Now he also wants him back. He says he

And by Tuesday, it's game over. Sam
Alman is returning as CEO of Open AI
nearly a week after he was fired by the
board.
>> The board folds. Toner and Macaulay step
down 120 hours. That's all it took for
Open AI to nearly self-destruct and then
somehow resurrect itself. But the story
doesn't end there because this wasn't
Altman's first controversial leadership

where it all started. Act two, the
original sin. What happens to a mission
when money shows up? Open AAI created so
many millionaires, but it was a

no profit motive. The manifesto was
clear. Our primary fiduciary duty is to

public. Research code breakthroughs.
Why? Because Google was going deep in
AI. And Elon and Alman had already seen

Sam and Elon became co-chairs. The

artificial general intelligence before

Google researchers introduced the

GPT1 could respond with such nuance,

2019, OpenAI realized that they needed

OpenAI LP. OpenAI became hiring

Altman decided not to take any equity

because it gave him something way more

The kind that comes from being selfless
or at least appearing selfless with the

an industry obsessed with stock grants
and exits, Sam Alman became the one with
nothing to lose. And that is real power.
As Cindy Looper once sang, money changes

2019 and 2023, Microsoft invested over

At that point, OpenAI stopped making all

Elon Musk said there was nothing open

over its predecessor. That's like going
from a go-kart to a rocket ship.
Training a model that size required
massive compute, and Microsoft had the
muscles to make it happen. And that's
the brutal math behind all these AI
models. You have to burn cash faster
than you can make it and you keep
burning it for a long time. Even today

heart of the conflict. OpenAI's

Altman, on the other hand, believed the

between doing what's right and doing

thought Altman repeatedly withheld
information from them. He didn't inform

and he met with some investors in the
Middle East to fund a new AI chip
without telling the board. To them, it

pattern and a dangerous one. According
to former OpenAI member Helen Toner,
this wasn't the first time Alman had

company, the management team had asked

The head of OpenAI's safety team

alignment that a model might optimize

that the brakes were failing at OpenAI.
Ultimate was and is very good at
consolidating power. Now, let's talk
about the platform. By May of 2025,
between 800 million and 1 billion people
were using Chat GPT every week. 92% of

AAI was valued at $300 billion. OpenAI

from to build AI hardware and maybe even

here's the paradox. Google called Chad
GPD a code red moment and internally
forced AI development into overdrive.
This was the first real threat to their
search dominance in over 20 years. And
they were in no mood to walk away.
Google didn't just respond, they went

around a strategy he calls AI
everywhere. Today over 1.5 billion users
interact with Google's AI layer.
Similarly, Meta made a hard pivot from
metaverse to AI. But it's good to
remember that the battle here isn't just
among these companies. It's a war on
philosophies. Open models like Llama
from Meta or Deepseek say make it free,
make it public and the transparency will
lead us to safety. But open-source

So they could be used or misused by bad
actors. And on the other hand, companies

we'll make sure bad actors don't misuse
this power. But it is undeniable that
this competitive spark that Alman lit is
now turned into a wildfire everywhere.
The rebels have become the empire. Each
one of us may see a different story in
this crisis. If we zoom out though, AI
may help us solve massive problems.
Better crops, cleaner energy, even

the hypothetical risk of rogue super
intelligent agents. For all the

intelligence, the biggest failure at
OpenAI wasn't technical. It was human.
The lessons may have very little to do
with artificial intelligence and almost
everything to do with human intelligence
because this is a story about human
relationships misfiring. Here are the
three reflections that I kept coming
back to. Number one, build relationships
before you need them. Alman was able to
regain control because of his
relationships, not his resume. When the
board fired him, it wasn't his coding

staying in touch, and showing up without
keeping score. I don't know Sam
personally, but I've met people like
that. The ones who build trust by giving

outcomes. It's been relationships that

roles may not last, but your skills

Dorsey can be ousted from the companies
they started, what makes any of us safe?
If you're pivoting careers, starting
one, upskilling, or building with AI,
this story might help you see where the

will still matter most. Here's what you
can try. First, protect your runway. If
you can, please save at least 6 to 12
months of expenses. The Open AI board
did not have a backup plan. You should.
It will buy you time to think, to
explore, to pivot, then start
experimenting. Can AI do part of your
job faster? Extend your creative output,
help you build something on the side,
help you think outside your comfort
zone. All of that is possible today. If
open AI story teaches us anything, is
this. Even the world's top experts don't
know how this movie ends. Don't be
threatened. Don't be intimidated. Keep
building. Number three, clear
communication can avoid a crisis. The
board had issues with Alman for months.
They didn't give him any clear feedback.
No structure, no signal, just
observation and silence. And that's why
when they finally acted, it looked like
a coup and not a course correction. It's
a familiar mistake. I've been in those
rooms where silence feels safer than
speaking the truth. I've made the same
mistakes of waiting too long. And I've
seen what it costs. Giving feedback to
powerful people is hard. But letting it
fester is actually harder. Because by
the time action is taken, trust is often
gone and even good decisions look like

try. Most of us avoid uncomfortable
conversations, but it's better to have
it early, have it clearly, have it with
care, and with compassion. One book that
changed how I approached this, Crucial
Conversations. Highly recommended.
That's the story of how a $300 billion
company nearly fell apart and what it

I love you.
